source_text:
Here’s one piece that brings them together — three American icons from completely different worlds, each famous for dominating their era in a way nobody else could.

### 1. Floyd Mayweather Jr. — "Money"

Floyd Mayweather isn't just a boxer, he's a business model with gloves on. Born in 1977 into a boxing family in Grand Rapids, Michigan, he turned pro in 1996 after winning bronze at the Atlanta Olympics.

What made him different wasn't power, it was precision. Mayweather fought as a defensive genius — the shoulder roll, the Philly Shell, the ability to make elite punchers miss by inches and pay for it immediately with a counter right hand.

The numbers are absurd: 50-0, 27 KOs, world champion in five weight classes from super featherweight (130 lbs) to light middleweight (154 lbs). He beat Genaro Hernández, Diego Corrales, Oscar De La Hoya, Ricky Hatton, Shane Mosley, Miguel Cotto, Canelo Álvarez, Manny Pacquiao, and Conor McGregor in his final fight.

Outside the ring he built the "Money" persona — flaunting wealth, promoting his own fights under Mayweather Promotions, and becoming the highest-paid athlete in the world multiple times without ever taking a loss. Critics call him boring or arrogant; fans call him the best pure boxer of his generation. Either way, he never left it to the judges by accident.

### 2. Mike Tyson — "Iron Mike"

If Mayweather was control, Mike Tyson was chaos.

Born in 1966 in Brooklyn, raised in Brownsville, Tyson was taken in as a teenager by trainer Cus D'Amato, who molded his rage into the peek-a-boo style — constant head movement, closing distance, devastating hooks and uppercuts.

At 20 years and 4 months, in 1986, he knocked out Trevor Berbick to become the youngest heavyweight champion in history — a record that still stands. For the next three years he was terrifying: 37-0, most fights ending in the first round, unifying the WBA, WBC, and IBF belts.

Tyson wasn't just winning, he was ending careers before the walk-in music stopped. Then came the fall — prison in 1992, the comeback, the infamous ear-bite fight with Evander Holyfield in 1997, bankruptcy, and years of personal turmoil.

That arc is why Tyson endures. He's not just a highlight reel of knockouts; he's a cautionary tale about fame, trauma, and reinvention. Today he's a pop-culture fixture — podcasts, acting cameos, cannabis business — still quoting Cus: "Fear is like fire. It can cook for you, or burn you."

### 3. Elvis Presley — "The King"

Twenty years before Tyson threw his first pro punch, a kid from Tupelo, Mississippi changed music forever.

Elvis Aaron Presley, born 1935, fused Black gospel, blues, and country into something white radio had never played that way. When he recorded "That's All Right" at Sun Records in 1954, then appeared on Ed Sullivan in 1956 shaking his hips, parents panicked and teenagers screamed.

He wasn't the first to sing rock and roll, but he was the first to make it national — and visual. Movies, jumpsuits, Las Vegas residencies, 33 films, over 1 billion records sold worldwide. Songs like "Heartbreak Hotel," "Suspicious Minds," "In the Ghetto," and "Can't Help Falling in Love" still define American pop.

Like Tyson, his peak was meteoric and his decline public — Army service, Hollywood years, prescription drug dependence, and his death at Graceland at just 42 in 1977. Like Mayweather, he understood branding before that word existed: the hair, the lip curl, the name "Elvis" needing no last name.

---

Put them side by side and you get the same story in three keys: Mayweather mastered defense and money, Tyson weaponized fear and power, Elvis weaponized sex appeal and sound. None of them were perfect men, all of them were perfect performers for their moment — and that's why, decades later, you still know exactly who they are after hearing just the first name.
